content:
**Executive summary:** Crops are classified by the season in which they are sown and harvested. Kharif crops — sown at the beginning of the monsoon (June–July); harvested by September–October.

**Must know**
• Crops are classified by the **season** in which they are sown and harvested.
• **Kharif crops** — sown at the **beginning of the monsoon** (June–July); harvested by **September–October**.
• – Also called **monsoon crops**.
• – Need **plenty of water and warmth**.
• – Examples: **rice, maize, cotton, jowar, bajra, groundnut, soybean, sugarcane**.
• **Rabi crops** — sown in **October/November** (beginning of winter); harvested by **March/April**.
• – Also called **winter crops**.
• – Need **cool climate** during growth; moderate water.
• – Examples: **wheat, barley, mustard, pea, gram, linseed, potato**.
• **Zaid crops** — short-season crops between rabi and kharif (summer): watermelon, cucumber, moong.
• India is an agricultural country — rice is the most important **kharif** crop; wheat is the most important **rabi** crop.
explanation:
**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• What is the main idea of "Kharif and Rabi Crops"?
• State: Crops are classified by the season in which they are sown and harvested.?
• Explain: Kharif crops?
• State: – Also called monsoon crops.?
• True or false trap: Mustard and pea are rabi, not kharif.?
• Exam focus: Give the sowing month, harvesting month AND two examples for each type — a three-part answ?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• **Kharif crop** — crop sown in the rainy season and harvested in autumn.
• **Rabi crop** — crop sown in winter and harvested in spring.
• Rice is **kharif**, wheat is **rabi** — the most commonly swapped pair.
• Mustard and pea are **rabi**, not kharif.
• Sowing time determines the category, not just the name of the crop.

**From linked questions (14 Qs):**
• KHARIF CROPS: Sown in early summer (around May), at the beginning of the first monsoon rains; known as autumn crops.
• Wheat is an important rabi crop of India, sown in October/early November.

**🔗 Links:** bio-rev-ch9-02 — next topic
teacherTip: Kharif = K for monsoon (rain). Rabi = R for winter. Rice needs rain → kharif. Wheat likes cool → rabi.
examTip: Give the sowing month, harvesting month AND two examples for each type — a three-part answer scores full marks.

content:
**Executive summary:** Manure — natural, organic material from decomposed plant and animal waste. – Examples: compost, farmyard manure (FYM), green manure, vermicompost.

**Must know**
• **Manure** — natural, organic material from decomposed plant and animal waste.
• – Examples: compost, farmyard manure (FYM), green manure, vermicompost.
• – Releases nutrients **slowly**; adds **humus**; improves soil texture, aeration and water-holding capacity.
• – Eco-friendly; no chemical pollution.
• **Fertilizers** — chemical/synthetic substances manufactured in factories.
• – Examples: urea, superphosphate, **NPK** (Nitrogen, Phosphorus, Potassium).
• – Release nutrients **quickly** in specific, measured amounts.
• – Do **not** improve soil structure; no humus added.
• – **Overuse** causes soil degradation, reduced fertility and **water pollution** (eutrophication).
• **NPK roles:** Nitrogen (N) → leaf/stem growth; Phosphorus (P) → root/flower/seed development; Potassium (K) → overall health and disease resistance.
• Best practice: use **both** — manure for long-term soil health, fertilizers for quick nutrient boost in correct doses.
explanation:
**📊 Chart — Manure vs Fertilizer**
| Feature | Manure | Fertilizer |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Source | Organic waste (cow dung, compost) | Chemical factories (NPK) |
| Nutrients | Less concentrated, slow release | High concentration, fast action |
| Soil health | Improves texture & microbes | No humus; excess harms soil |


**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• What is the main idea of "Manure and Fertilizers"?
• Explain: Manure?
• State: – Examples: compost, farmyard manure (FYM), green manure, vermicompost.?
• State: – Releases nutrients slowly; adds humus; improves soil texture, aeration an?
• True or false trap: Fertilizers are NOT always better than manure — overuse damages soil.?
• True or false trap: K in NPK = Potassium (from Latin Kalium), not 'keratin' or 'kalium' alone.?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• **Manure** — organic matter used to enrich soil.
• **Fertilizer** — a chemical substance supplying specific plant nutrients.
• **Humus** — dark, decomposed organic matter that improves soil fertility.
• Fertilizers are NOT always better than manure — overuse damages soil.
• K in NPK = **Potassium** (from Latin Kalium), not 'keratin' or 'kalium' alone.
• Manure improves soil **structure**; fertilizers only add nutrients.

**From linked questions (14 Qs):**
• MANURE: • Natural/organic material (decomposed plant and animal waste) • Provides nutrients slowly over time • Improves soil structure and water-holding capacit
• Organic farming is the practice of raising crops without using synthetic fertilisers and pesticides.

**🔗 Links:** bio-rev-ch9-01 — previous topic; bio-rev-ch9-03 — next topic
teacherTip: Manure = slow, natural, builds soil. Fertilizer = fast, chemical, feeds plant only. Compare in a table for distinguish questions.
examTip: Compare at least five features (source, speed, humus, soil structure, pollution) — ICSE distinguish answers need a full table.

content:
**Executive summary:** Organic farming — a method of agriculture that avoids synthetic chemical fertilizers and pesticides; relies on natural i. Key practices:.

**Must know**
• **Organic farming** — a method of agriculture that avoids synthetic **chemical fertilizers and pesticides**; relies on natural inputs.
• Key practices:
• – Use of **manure, compost and vermicompost** for soil fertility.
• – **Crop rotation** — growing different crops in succession to maintain soil nutrients and break pest cycles.
• – **Biological pest control** — using natural predators (ladybird beetles eat aphids) instead of chemical pesticides.
• – **Green manure** — growing plants like sunn hemp and ploughing them into the soil.
• – **Intercropping** — growing two or more crops together (e.g. maize + beans).
• **Advantages:** healthier food (no chemical residues), protects soil and water, sustainable long-term, preserves biodiversity.
• **Disadvantages:** lower yield initially, more labour-intensive, slower pest control, produce may be costlier.
• Organic produce is in high demand for health-conscious consumers.
explanation:
**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• What is the main idea of "Organic Farming"?
• Explain: Organic farming?
• State: – Use of manure, compost and vermicompost for soil fertility.?
• True or false trap: Organic farming does NOT mean 'no pest control' — it uses biological methods instead ?
• True or false trap: Organic farming still uses fertilizers — but organic ones (manure, compost), not chem?
• Exam focus: List advantages AND disadvantages — balanced answers show deeper understanding and earn hi?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• **Organic farming** — farming without synthetic chemicals, using ecological principles.
• **Crop rotation** — alternating different crops on the same land across seasons.
• Organic farming does NOT mean 'no pest control' — it uses **biological** methods instead of chemicals.
• Organic farming still uses fertilizers — but **organic** ones (manure, compost), not chemical NPK.
• Crop rotation prevents soil **nutrient depletion**, not just pest build-up.

**From linked questions (14 Qs):**
• MANURE: • Natural/organic material (decomposed plant and animal waste) • Provides nutrients slowly over time • Improves soil structure and water-holding capacit
• Organic farming is the practice of raising crops without using synthetic fertilisers and pesticides.

**🔗 Links:** bio-rev-ch9-02 — previous topic; bio-rev-ch9-04 — next topic
teacherTip: Organic = nature's way: compost not chemicals, ladybirds not pesticides, rotation not monoculture.
examTip: List advantages AND disadvantages — balanced answers show deeper understanding and earn higher marks.

content:
**Executive summary:** Animal husbandry — the scientific rearing and management of livestock (domestic animals) for food, fibre and other produ. Essential care for all livestock: proper shelter, balanced feed, clean water, disease prevention (va.

**Must know**
• **Animal husbandry** — the scientific rearing and management of livestock (domestic animals) for food, fibre and other products.
• Essential care for all livestock: proper **shelter**, **balanced feed**, **clean water**, **disease prevention** (vaccination, deworming) and selective **breeding**.
• Main branches:
• – **Dairy farming** — rearing milch animals (cows, buffaloes, goats) for milk, butter, cheese, ghee.
• – **Poultry farming** — rearing birds (hens, ducks) for eggs and meat.
• – **Piggery** — rearing pigs for pork.
• – **Sheep/goat rearing** — wool, meat (mutton), milk.
• **Indigenous breeds** (India): Sahiwal, Gir (cows); Aseel (poultry); Murrah, Mehsana (buffaloes).
• **Exotic breeds** (foreign): Jersey, Brown Swiss (cows); White Leghorn, Rhode Island Red (fowl).
• **Cattle feed:** roughage (fodder, hay — bulk/fibre) and concentrates (cereals, wheat — nutrient-rich).
• **Draught animals** — bullocks, camels, horses, elephants — reared for heavy farm work, not food.
explanation:
**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• What is the main idea of "Animal Husbandry"?
• Explain: Animal husbandry?
• State: Essential care for all livestock: proper shelter, balanced feed, clean wate?
• True or false trap: Draught animals are for work, not specifically found only in low-rainfall areas.?
• Exam focus: Name the branch (dairy/poultry/piggery), the product obtained, and one breed example for f?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• **Animal husbandry** — scientific management of farm animals.
• **Milch animal** — an animal kept primarily for milk production.
• **Livestock** — domesticated animals raised on a farm.
• Sahiwal and Gir are **indigenous** Indian breeds; Jersey is **exotic** (foreign).
• Draught animals are for **work**, not specifically found only in low-rainfall areas.
• Concentrates are nutrient-rich feed (cereals), not roughage (fodder).

**From linked questions (14 Qs):**
• (a) Animal husbandry — the branch of biology dealing with the feeding, shelter, care and breeding of domesticated animals.

**🔗 Links:** bio-rev-ch9-03 — previous topic; bio-rev-ch9-05 — next topic
teacherTip: Animal husbandry = farming animals scientifically. Know one indigenous and one exotic breed each for cows and poultry — exam favourites.
examTip: Name the branch (dairy/poultry/piggery), the product obtained, and one breed example for full marks.

content:
**Executive summary:** Apiculture — rearing of honey bees (*Apis indica* — common Indian honey bee) for honey and beeswax. – Bees pollinate flowers → increase crop yield (bonus benefit).

**Must know**
• **Apiculture** — rearing of **honey bees** (*Apis indica* — common Indian honey bee) for **honey and beeswax**.
• – Bees pollinate flowers → increase crop yield (bonus benefit).
• – Honey is a natural sweetener with medicinal value.
• **Sericulture** — rearing of **silkworms** (*Bombyx mori*) for production of **silk**.
• – Silkworm larva spins a **cocoon** of silk thread.
• – **Reeling** — process of recovering silk threads from cocoons (larvae are killed during this process).
• – India is a major silk producer; sericulture provides rural employment.
• **Pisciculture (fish culture / aquaculture)** — rearing of **fish** in ponds, tanks or rivers for food.
• – Common edible Indian fish: **rohu, catla, hilsa, Bombay duck, eel**.
• – Fish is rich in **protein** and omega-3 fatty acids.
• All three are specialised branches of animal husbandry with economic importance.
explanation:
**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• What is the main idea of "Apiculture, Sericulture and Pisciculture"?
• Explain: Apiculture?
• State: – Bees pollinate flowers → increase crop yield (bonus benefit).?
• State: – Honey is a natural sweetener with medicinal value.?
• True or false trap: Apiculture = bees, NOT fish — a standard true/false trap.?
• True or false trap: Silk comes from the cocoon spun by the larva, not directly from the larva's body.?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• **Apiculture** — bee-keeping (Latin *apis* = bee).
• **Sericulture** — silk farming (Latin *sericum* = silk).
• **Pisciculture** — fish farming (Latin *piscis* = fish).
• Apiculture = **bees**, NOT fish — a standard true/false trap.
• Silk comes from the **cocoon** spun by the larva, not directly from the larva's body.
• Pisciculture and aquaculture mean the same thing.

**From linked questions (14 Qs):**
• (a) Animal husbandry — the branch of biology dealing with the feeding, shelter, care and breeding of domesticated animals.
• (a) The four stages are: egg, larva, pupa, adult.
• Incorrect statement: Organic farming and green revolution in brief (awareness level).
• GREEN REVOLUTION benefits: 1.

**🔗 Links:** bio-rev-ch9-04 — previous topic; bio-rev-ch9-06 — next topic
teacherTip: Api = bee. Seri = silk. Pisci = fish. Learn the Latin roots and you will never confuse the three.
examTip: Match column questions always pair: Apiculture→bees, Sericulture→silkworms, Pisciculture→fishes. Write the scientific name when asked.

content:
**Executive summary:** Vermiculture — the practice of raising earthworms (*Eisenia fetida* / red wigglers) for converting organic waste into va. Vermicomposting — the process in which earthworms eat decomposing plant/animal waste and excrete nut.

**Must know**
• **Vermiculture** — the practice of raising **earthworms** (*Eisenia fetida* / red wigglers) for converting organic waste into valuable **vermicompost**.
• **Vermicomposting** — the process in which earthworms eat decomposing plant/animal waste and excrete nutrient-rich **castings** (vermicompost).
• **Advantages of vermicompost:**
• – Rich in **N, P, K** and micronutrients.
• – Improves soil structure, aeration and water retention.
• – Speeds up decomposition compared to ordinary composting.
• – Eco-friendly waste recycling — reduces landfill.
• – Free of harmful pathogens and weed seeds.
• Earthworms also **aerate the soil** by burrowing, helping root growth.
• Vermicompost is a premium organic manure used in organic farming and kitchen gardens.
• Setup: shallow pits or bins with moist organic waste, earthworms, and a cover to maintain humidity.
explanation:
**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• What is the main idea of "Vermiculture and Vermicomposting"?
• Explain: Vermiculture?
• Explain: Vermicomposting?
• State: Advantages of vermicompost:?
• True or false trap: Vermicompost is a type of manure, not a chemical fertilizer.?
• True or false trap: Vermiculture uses earthworms, not insects or bacteria alone.?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• **Vermiculture** — culture (rearing) of earthworms.
• **Vermicompost** — compost produced by the digestive activity of earthworms.
• Vermicompost is a type of **manure**, not a chemical fertilizer.
• Vermiculture uses **earthworms**, not insects or bacteria alone.
• Do not confuse vermiculture with sericulture (silkworms) or apiculture (bees).

**From linked questions (14 Qs):**
• MANURE: • Natural/organic material (decomposed plant and animal waste) • Provides nutrients slowly over time • Improves soil structure and water-holding capacit
• (a) Animal husbandry — the branch of biology dealing with the feeding, shelter, care and breeding of domesticated animals.
• Incorrect statement: Organic farming and green revolution in brief (awareness level).
• Organic farming is the practice of raising crops without using synthetic fertilisers and pesticides.

**🔗 Links:** bio-rev-ch9-05 — previous topic; bio-rev-ch9-07 — next topic
teacherTip: Worms eat garbage, poop gold (for soil). Vermicompost = worm castings = premium organic manure.
examTip: List the advantages of vermicompost over ordinary manure — nutrient richness and faster decomposition are key points.

content:
**Executive summary:** Green Revolution (1960s–70s) — a programme to increase food grain (crop) production in India using:. – High-yielding variety (HYV) seeds (wheat, rice).

**Must know**
• **Green Revolution** (1960s–70s) — a programme to increase **food grain (crop) production** in India using:
• – High-yielding variety (HYV) seeds (wheat, rice).
• – Modern irrigation (tubewells, canals).
• – Chemical fertilizers and pesticides.
• – Mechanised farming (tractors, harvesters).
• – Led by **Dr M. S. Swaminathan** ('Father of Green Revolution in India').
• – Result: India became **self-sufficient in food grains**.
• **White Revolution (Operation Flood)** — a programme to increase **milk production** in India:
• – Established a national milk grid linking dairy cooperatives.
• – Improved cattle breeds, veterinary care and milk processing.
• – Led by **Dr Verghese Kurien** ('Father of White Revolution in India') at **Amul**, Anand (Gujarat).
• – Result: India became the **world's largest milk producer**.
• Green = crops/grains. White = milk. Both transformed India's food security.
explanation:
**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• What is the main idea of "Green Revolution and White Revolution"?
• Explain: Green Revolution (1960s–70s)?
• State: – High-yielding variety (HYV) seeds (wheat, rice).?
• State: – Modern irrigation (tubewells, canals).?
• True or false trap: Green Revolution used chemical fertilizers — it is not the same as organic farming.?
• Exam focus: Name the leader, the product increased, and one method used — three facts per revolution f?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• **Green Revolution** — adoption of modern agricultural techniques to boost crop yields.
• **White Revolution** — programme for rapid increase in milk production through cooperatives.
• Green Revolution = **crops** (Swaminathan); White Revolution = **milk** (Kurien) — never swap the leaders or products.
• Green Revolution used **chemical** fertilizers — it is not the same as organic farming.
• Operation Flood is the other name for the White Revolution.

**From linked questions (14 Qs):**
• GREEN REVOLUTION: Involved high-yielding crop varieties, enhanced irrigation and the use of chemical fertilisers and pesticides to increase agricultural (food grain) productivity.
• GREEN REVOLUTION benefits: 1.

**🔗 Links:** bio-rev-ch9-06 — previous topic; bio-rev-ch9-08 — next topic
teacherTip: Green = fields (Swaminathan). White = milk (Kurien/Amul). Two revolutions, two heroes, two products.
examTip: Name the leader, the product increased, and one method used — three facts per revolution for a complete answer.

subtopic: 8. Useful Bacteria in the Food Industry
content:
**Executive summary:** Many bacteria are useful in food production and processing:. – *Lactobacillus acidophilus* — converts milk sugar (lactose) into lactic acid, curdling milk into c.

**Must know**
• Many bacteria are **useful** in food production and processing:
• – ***Lactobacillus acidophilus*** — converts milk sugar (lactose) into **lactic acid**, curdling milk into **curd**; also used in cheese-making.
• – ***Rhizobium*** — **nitrogen-fixing** bacteria in root nodules of legumes (pea, bean); enriches soil with nitrogen → improves crop yield.
• – Bacteria in **curing** of **tea leaves** and **tobacco** — produce specific flavours and aroma through controlled fermentation/drying.
• – Bacteria in **retting** of jute stems — separate fibres from the stem.
• – Bacteria in **tanning** of leather and **production of vinegar**.
• **Yeast** (*Saccharomyces*) — a fungus (not bacterium) used in **bakeries** (bread rises) and **breweries** (alcohol fermentation).
• Curing of tobacco is done by **bacteria**, NOT yeast — a common MCQ trap.
• These microbes are examples of **beneficial** microorganisms in agriculture and industry.
explanation:
**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• What is the main idea of "Useful Bacteria in the Food Industry"?
• State: Many bacteria are useful in food production and processing:?
• Explain: – *Lactobacillus acidophilus*?
• Explain: – *Rhizobium*?
• True or false trap: Lactobacillus makes curd, not cottage cheese directly — further processing makes chee?
• Exam focus: Give the scientific name in italics (Lactobacillus acidophilus) and the product — binomial?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• **Fermentation** — breakdown of organic substances by microorganisms in the absence of oxygen.
• **Nitrogen fixation** — conversion of atmospheric nitrogen into usable compounds by bacteria.
• **Curing** — controlled fermentation/drying of leaves to develop flavour.
• Lactobacillus makes **curd**, not cottage cheese directly — further processing makes cheese.
• Yeast is used in bakeries and breweries, but **not** in tobacco curing.
• Rhizobium fixes nitrogen — **fungi do not** fix nitrogen.

**From linked questions (14 Qs):**
• Uses of bacteria in the food industry: 1.

**🔗 Links:** bio-rev-ch9-07 — previous topic; bio-rev-ch9-09 — next topic
teacherTip: Lactobacillus = curd. Rhizobium = nitrogen in soil. Bacteria cure tea/tobacco. Yeast bakes bread — four microbes, four jobs.
examTip: Give the scientific name in italics (Lactobacillus acidophilus) and the product — binomial names earn extra marks.

content:
**Executive summary:** Agriculture — the science and practice of cultivating soil, growing crops and raising livestock. Seven steps of crop production:.

**Must know**
• **Agriculture** — the science and practice of cultivating soil, growing crops and raising livestock.
• **Seven steps of crop production:**
• 1. **Preparation of soil** — ploughing (tilling), levelling, manuring; loosens soil for air and roots.
• 2. **Sowing** — selecting good **seeds** (healthy, viable); sowing at correct depth and spacing; methods: broadcasting, drilling, transplantation (paddy in orchards).
• 3. **Adding manure and fertilizers** — supply nutrients (N, P, K).
• 4. **Irrigation** — supplying water at right intervals; methods: wells, tubewells, canals, drip irrigation.
• 5. **Weeding** — removing unwanted plants (weeds) that compete for nutrients, water and light; manual removal or **pesticides/herbicides**.
• 6. **Harvesting** — cutting the mature crop; manual (sickle) or mechanical (combine harvester).
• 7. **Storage** — drying grains, storing in **granaries/silos** protected from moisture, pests and rodents.
• **Crop protection:** pesticides (kill pests), fungicides (kill fungi), rodenticides (kill rodents).
explanation:
**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• What is the main idea of "Crop Production — Steps and Practices"?
• Explain: Agriculture?
• State: Seven steps of crop production:?
• Explain: 1. Preparation of soil?
• True or false trap: Sowing comes AFTER soil preparation, not before.?
• True or false trap: Transplantation is used for paddy and orchard crops, not for wheat.?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• **Ploughing** — turning and loosening the soil before sowing.
• **Weed** — an unwanted plant growing along with the crop.
• **Harvesting** — cutting and gathering the mature crop.
• Sowing comes AFTER soil preparation, not before.
• Transplantation is used for **paddy** and orchard crops, not for wheat.
• Storage must protect from **moisture** — damp grain rots and germinates.

**From linked questions (14 Qs):**
• Incorrect statement: Without microbes, the earth would soon be covered with the dead organisms.
• MANURE: • Natural/organic material (decomposed plant and animal waste) • Provides nutrients slowly over time • Improves soil structure and water-holding capacit
• GREEN REVOLUTION benefits: 1.

**🔗 Links:** bio-rev-ch9-08 — previous topic; bio-rev-ch9-10 — next topic
teacherTip: Prepare → Sow → Nourish → Water → Weed → Harvest → Store. Seven steps in order — write them as a numbered list every time.
examTip: If asked to 'describe crop production', write all seven steps with one detail each — skipping a step loses marks.

subtopic: 10. Yeast, Mushrooms and Microbes in Agriculture
content:
**Executive summary:** Yeast (*Saccharomyces cerevisiae*) — a unicellular fungus used in:. – Bakeries — ferments sugar in dough, producing CO₂ that makes bread rise (soft and porous).

**Must know**
• **Yeast** (*Saccharomyces cerevisiae*) — a unicellular fungus used in:
• – **Bakeries** — ferments sugar in dough, producing CO₂ that makes bread **rise** (soft and porous).
• – **Breweries** — ferments grain sugars to produce **alcohol** (beer, wine).
• **Mushrooms** (*Agaricus*) — edible fungi grown in **mushroom farms** (dark, humid, cool conditions); rich in protein; example of fungal food.
• **Antibiotics from fungi** — *Penicillium* produces **penicillin** (used against bacterial infections).
• **Composting** — bacteria and fungi decompose organic waste into compost (natural manure).
• **Biofertilizers** — living microorganisms (Rhizobium, Azotobacter, blue-green algae) that enrich soil nutrients naturally.
• **Silage** — fermented fodder stored for cattle feed during dry seasons; fermentation by bacteria.
• Understanding useful vs harmful microbes is essential for sustainable agriculture.
explanation:
**Cornell cues (self-test):**
• What is the main idea of "Yeast, Mushrooms and Microbes in Agriculture"?
• Explain: Yeast (*Saccharomyces cerevisiae*)?
• Explain: – Bakeries?
• Explain: – Breweries?
• True or false trap: Yeast is a fungus, not a bacterium — do not group it with Lactobacillus.?
• Exam focus: For 'uses of microbes in food industry', give at least one bacterium (Lactobacillus) AND o?

**Definitions / Exam traps:**
• **Fermentation** — anaerobic breakdown of sugars by yeast into alcohol and CO₂.
• **Biofertilizer** — a substance containing living microorganisms that promote plant growth.
• Yeast is a **fungus**, not a bacterium — do not group it with Lactobacillus.
• Yeast produces CO₂ in baking and alcohol in brewing — both are fermentation but different products.
• Penicillin comes from the fungus *Penicillium*, not from bacteria.

**From linked questions (14 Qs):**
• MANURE: • Natural/organic material (decomposed plant and animal waste) • Provides nutrients slowly over time • Improves soil structure and water-holding capacit

**🔗 Links:** bio-rev-ch9-09 — previous topic
teacherTip: Yeast = baker's and brewer's friend (fungus, not bacterium). Bread rises from CO₂; beer comes from alcohol — same yeast, different recipes.
examTip: For 'uses of microbes in food industry', give at least one bacterium (Lactobacillus) AND one fungus (yeast) with their specific products.
